answers.field_label: A1 How Long Have You Been With The Agency
answers.value: I have been with the Riverview County Department of Children and Family Services for just over 17 years (hired in 2008-06, so 17+ years as of the date of this assessment). I started as a front-line social worker and have held progressively responsible positions since then, including supervisor, program manager, and now deputy director.

answers.value:
At the time our agency first became involved with the Family to Family initiative through Annie E. Casey (around 2010), I was a Senior Children’s Social Worker assigned to our Emergency Response unit. In that role I carried a caseload of high-risk referrals and participated in team decision-making meetings with families. By the time we joined the California Disproportionality Project (CDP) in 2013, I had been promoted to Supervising Children’s Social Worker overseeing a mixed Emergency Response/Family Maintenance unit. I supervised 8–10 line staff, was responsible for unit-level outcomes (including response times, placement stability, and re-entry rates), and served as the primary liaison between my unit and our internal training division.

As our REI and anti-racism work deepened, I took on additional responsibilities. In 2016 I became a Program Manager for Permanency and Placement, where I oversaw three units: Resource Family Approval, Concurrent Planning, and Relative/NREFM placement. In that capacity I was directly responsible for reviewing data on racial disproportionality and disparate outcomes in foster care placements, and for proposing practice changes related to kin-first placement and culturally responsive case planning. Since 2021, in my current role as Deputy Director of Child Welfare Services, I have executive oversight of our child welfare operations, including policy development, quality improvement, and the integration of race equity and inclusion (REI) principles into all aspects of our work. I sit on the agency’s Executive Leadership Team, co-chair the REI Steering Committee, and provide regular updates to the County Board of Supervisors on our progress regarding racial equity indicators.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
My involvement in anti-racism and REI initiatives has evolved over time.

During the early phase of our participation in Family to Family (2010–2012), as a front-line worker, I served as a team member on several workgroups focused on family engagement and community partnerships. I helped pilot family team meetings in our region, facilitated safety and strengths-based conversations with parents and kin, and provided feedback on how the new practices were affecting day-to-day casework. I also participated in a small internal group of Spanish-speaking staff who reviewed outreach materials to ensure they were culturally and linguistically appropriate for our Latino families.

When we joined the California Disproportionality Project (CDP) in 2013, I was a supervisor and took on a more formal leadership role. I was selected as one of our county’s CDP Site Leads for the front-line practice component. In that role, I:
- Attended statewide CDP convenings and brought tools and learning back to the county.
- Co-facilitated staff trainings on implicit bias, cultural humility, and structured decision-making (SDM) with a racial equity lens.
- Led unit-level data reviews where we looked at referral, substantiation, and removal rates by race/ethnicity and discussed specific cases where decision-making might have been influenced by bias.
- Helped design and co-facilitate “courageous conversations” sessions with staff, using the four agreements of courageous conversations about race (stay engaged, speak your truth, experience discomfort, expect and accept non-closure).

As a Program Manager (2016–2021), my project role expanded. I co-chaired the Permanency and Placement Equity Workgroup, which focused specifically on reducing racial disparities in placement type, placement stability, and time to permanency. Concrete responsibilities included:
- Reviewing quarterly data disaggregated by race/ethnicity to identify trends (e.g., Black and Native American youth being placed in higher levels of care and experiencing more placement moves).
- Working with our data unit to develop dashboards that made these disparities visible to supervisors and line staff.
- Partnering with community-based organizations led by Black, Native, and Latino community members to co-design kinship support services.
- Drafting revisions to our placement and visitation policies to prioritize kin-first placement, maintain sibling connections, and ensure that cultural and religious practices were supported in foster homes.

In my current role as Deputy Director (2021–present), I serve as one of the executive sponsors for our agency-wide REI initiative. As an executive sponsor, I:
- Co-chair the REI Steering Committee, which includes staff from all levels of the organization as well as community partners and youth/parent representatives with lived experience.
- Oversee the integration of race equity goals into our strategic plan, including specific, measurable objectives tied to reducing disproportionality and disparity.
- Champion the allocation of resources (budget, staff time, and training) to sustain the work beyond short-term grants.
- Present regularly to the County Board of Supervisors and our Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention Commission on key REI indicators and progress.
- Mentor emerging leaders of color within the department and support affinity groups (e.g., African American Staff Association, Latinx Staff Circle) in having a voice in policy and practice changes.

On a more personal level, as a Latina, bilingual (English/Spanish) leader who grew up in a low-income neighborhood in this same county, I bring my lived experience to the work. I often share with staff that my own family had involvement with child welfare when I was a teenager, and that experience shapes my commitment to ensuring that our systems do not replicate historical harms or treat families of color as inherently deficient. I have used that personal history in trainings and listening sessions to help normalize honest conversations about race, power, and trust between the agency and the communities we serve.

answers.field_label: D Contact Information For Other Person People
answers.value:
1. Name: Dr. Samuel K. Thompson
   Role: Former Director, Riverview County Department of Children and Family Services (served 2011–2019); original executive sponsor for Family to Family and CDP initiatives.
   Phone: (555) 274-9031
   Email: samuel.thompson.consulting@gmail.com
   Notes: Although retired from the county, Dr. Thompson currently consults with several California counties on race equity in child welfare. He can speak in detail about the decision-making process that led Riverview County to join both Family to Family and the California Disproportionality Project, as well as the political and community context at the time.

2. Name: Angela M. Chen, LCSW
   Role: REI Program Manager, Riverview County DCFS (current); former frontline social worker and CDP site team member.
   Phone: (555) 613-4880
   Email: angela.chen@riverviewcounty.gov
   Notes: Angela coordinated much of the day-to-day work related to CDP, including organizing staff trainings, facilitating focus groups with birth parents, and maintaining our local REI action plan. She has institutional knowledge of how the initiative was received by staff at different levels and how we attempted to sustain the work after the initial grant period.

3. Name: Mariah J. Little
   Role: Parent Partner Coordinator, Families First Collaborative (community-based organization contracted with Riverview County DCFS); served as a community representative on the REI Steering Committee.
   Phone: (555) 489-7721
   Email: mlittle@familiesfirstcollab.org
   Notes: Mariah is a Black mother with lived experience in the child welfare system who later became a certified parent partner. She was instrumental in pushing the agency to examine how racism and bias affected initial hotline calls, safety assessments, and removal decisions. She can provide a candid perspective on the strengths and limitations of the county’s previous anti-racism efforts, particularly from the viewpoint of families directly impacted by the system.
